cards.title: Integrative Oncology — Principles & Evidence

alerts.label: Definition (SIO)
alerts.text: Integrative oncology combines evidence-based complementary approaches with conventional cancer treatment to improve quality of life, reduce side effects, and optimise outcomes. It does NOT replace conventional treatment.

rows: Chemotherapy-induced nausea ★, cancer pain, hot flushes
Chemo side effects ↓, sleep, possible survival benefit
Survival association in colorectal, breast, prostate Ca

pearls.label: 🟡 Exercise in cancer — the evidence
pearls.text: Exercise reduces breast cancer recurrence by 40% and colorectal cancer recurrence by 50% in RCT/cohort data. 150 min moderate or 75 min vigorous/week is the threshold. Mechanism: ↓ IGF-1, ↓ oestrogen, ↓ insulin, ↑ NK cells, ↑ tumour perfusion (improves chemo delivery). Cancer-related fatigue: exercise is more effective than any pharmacological intervention. Prescribe it.

cards._legacyHtml:
Definition (SIO)
Integrative oncology combines evidence-based complementary approaches with conventional cancer treatment to improve quality of life, reduce side effects, and optimise outcomes. It does NOT replace conventional treatment.
InterventionEvidence LevelApplicationMechanism
AcupunctureGrade AChemotherapy-induced nausea ★, cancer pain, hot flushesEndorphin release, autonomic modulation
Mind-body (yoga, MBSR)Grade AFatigue ★, anxiety, sleep, QoLHPA axis, immune modulation, cortisol ↓
ExerciseGrade ACancer-related fatigue ★, recurrence risk ↓, survival ↑IGF-1 ↓, inflammation ↓, NK cell ↑
MelatoninGrade BChemo side effects ↓, sleep, possible survival benefitAntioxidant, anti-proliferative, immune
Mistletoe (Iscador)Grade BQoL ↑, fatigue ↓, immune modulationNK cell activation, apoptosis induction
Vitamin D (if deficient)Grade BSurvival association in colorectal, breast, prostate CaAnti-proliferative, immune modulation
GingerGrade BCINV (chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting)5-HT3 antagonism, gastrokinetic
🟡 Exercise in cancer — the evidence
Exercise reduces breast cancer recurrence by 40% and colorectal cancer recurrence by 50% in RCT/cohort data. 150 min moderate or 75 min vigorous/week is the threshold. Mechanism: ↓ IGF-1, ↓ oestrogen, ↓ insulin, ↑ NK cells, ↑ tumour perfusion (improves chemo delivery). Cancer-related fatigue: exercise is more effective than any pharmacological intervention. Prescribe it.

cards.title: Oncology Nutrition — Evidence-Based

rows: ↓ Overall cancer incidence ★; strongest for colorectal, breast, endometrial
↓ Lung, colon, breast cancer; indole-3-carbinol modulates oestrogen
Protective in breast Ca (population data) — controversial in BRCA+
Pre-clinical strong, clinical trials ongoing; safe adjunct
Fasting 24–72h before chemo ↓ side effects, ↑ efficacy (pilot data)
IGF-1 ↓, differential stress response (normal vs cancer cells)

pearls.label: 🟡 What to avoid in oncology
pearls.text: High-dose antioxidants (Vit C, Vit E, beta-carotene) during chemotherapy — may protect cancer cells from oxidative damage of chemo/RT. St John's Wort with any chemotherapy — CYP3A4 induction reduces drug levels. High-dose green tea extract with bortezomib (direct antagonism — clinical trial stopped). Always check herb-chemo interactions at oncologydatabaseinteraction.com or Memorial Sloan Kettering's About Herbs database.

cards._legacyHtml:
Dietary patternCancer evidenceMechanism
Mediterranean diet↓ Overall cancer incidence ★; strongest for colorectal, breast, endometrialAnti-inflammatory, antioxidant, gut microbiome, IGF-1 ↓
Plant-rich diet↓ Colorectal cancer (fibre), ↓ processed meat associationButyrate, phytochemicals, transit time ↓
Cruciferous vegetables↓ Lung, colon, breast cancer; indole-3-carbinol modulates oestrogenSulforaphane (Nrf2), I3C → DIM (oestrogen metabolism)
Soy (isoflavones)Protective in breast Ca (population data) — controversial in BRCA+SERM-like activity — context dependent
Turmeric/curcuminPre-clinical strong, clinical trials ongoing; safe adjunctNF-κB, apoptosis, anti-angiogenic
Caloric restriction / fastingFasting 24–72h before chemo ↓ side effects, ↑ efficacy (pilot data)IGF-1 ↓, differential stress response (normal vs cancer cells)
🟡 What to avoid in oncology
High-dose antioxidants (Vit C, Vit E, beta-carotene) during chemotherapy — may protect cancer cells from oxidative damage of chemo/RT. St John's Wort with any chemotherapy — CYP3A4 induction reduces drug levels. High-dose green tea extract with bortezomib (direct antagonism — clinical trial stopped). Always check herb-chemo interactions at oncologydatabaseinteraction.com or Memorial Sloan Kettering's About Herbs database.

cards.title: Herb & Supplement Safety in Cancer Treatment

alerts.label: Critical rule
alerts.text: ALL high-dose antioxidant supplements should be paused 48 hours before and during chemotherapy/radiotherapy unless specifically approved by the oncologist. This is the single most important safety rule in integrative oncology.

rows: May antagonise oxidative mechanism of some chemo drugs. Also possibly synergistic — highly context-dependent. Do NOT recommend without oncologist consent.
CYP3A4 induction → ↓ irinotecan, imatinib, tamoxifen, taxol levels by 30–50%
Antagonises bortezomib (boronic acid-EGCG binding). Moderate doses of green tea as beverage: safe.
Theoretical oestrogenic effect in hormone-sensitive breast/endo cancer — avoid supplements; dietary soy appears safe
Generally safe, potentially synergistic (radiosensitiser) — check with oncologist

cards._legacyHtml:
Critical rule
ALL high-dose antioxidant supplements should be paused 48 hours before and during chemotherapy/radiotherapy unless specifically approved by the oncologist. This is the single most important safety rule in integrative oncology.
SupplementConcern with chemotherapyEvidence level
Vitamin C (high-dose IV/oral)May antagonise oxidative mechanism of some chemo drugs. Also possibly synergistic — highly context-dependent. Do NOT recommend without oncologist consent.Conflicting
St John's WortCYP3A4 induction → ↓ irinotecan, imatinib, tamoxifen, taxol levels by 30–50%Contraindicated ★
Green tea extract (EGCG)Antagonises bortezomib (boronic acid-EGCG binding). Moderate doses of green tea as beverage: safe.Avoid with bortezomib
Soy isoflavonesTheoretical oestrogenic effect in hormone-sensitive breast/endo cancer — avoid supplements; dietary soy appears safeDietary safe, supplements avoid
MelatoninGenerally safe, potentially synergistic (radiosensitiser) — check with oncologistLikely safe

cards.title: Cancer Cachexia — Naturopathic Nutritional Support

alerts.label: Definition
alerts.text: Cancer cachexia = metabolic syndrome of muscle wasting (sarcopenia), anorexia, inflammation and fat loss driven by tumour-host interactions. Present in 80% of advanced cancer. Accounts for 20–30% of cancer deaths directly.

rows: Weight loss >5% (or BMI <20 + >2% loss, or sarcopenia + >2% loss)
Performance status 3–4, progressive disease, life expectancy <3 months
Grade A — 1.5–2.0g/kg/day to preserve lean mass
Leucine-rich sources (whey protein, legumes, fish). Small frequent meals.
Grade A — EPA 2g/day attenuates muscle wasting
Fish oil supplement. EPA preferentially over DHA. Liquid form for poor appetite.
Grade B — improves intake and QoL
High-calorie, high-protein. Fortisip, Ensure Plus. Small volume, high density.
Grade B — anabolic stimulus, preserves muscle

pearls.label: 🟡 EPA — the anti-cachectic omega-3
pearls.text: Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), not DHA, is the key anti-cachectic omega-3. Mechanism: ↓ PIF (proteolysis-inducing factor), ↓ NF-κB, ↓ muscle ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Dose: 2g EPA/day from fish oil. Clinical trials show stabilisation of weight and lean mass in pancreatic and lung cancer cachexia. Liquid fish oil is better tolerated when appetite is poor.

cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Cancer cachexia = metabolic syndrome of muscle wasting (sarcopenia), anorexia, inflammation and fat loss driven by tumour-host interactions. Present in 80% of advanced cancer. Accounts for 20–30% of cancer deaths directly.
StageFeaturesIntervention window
Pre-cachexiaWeight loss <5%, anorexia, metabolic changesBest intervention window — preventive
CachexiaWeight loss >5% (or BMI <20 + >2% loss, or sarcopenia + >2% loss)Multimodal — nutrition + exercise + pharmacology
Refractory cachexiaPerformance status 3–4, progressive disease, life expectancy <3 monthsComfort and dignity focus
Nutritional interventionEvidenceProtocol
Protein ★Grade A — 1.5–2.0g/kg/day to preserve lean massLeucine-rich sources (whey protein, legumes, fish). Small frequent meals.
Omega-3 (EPA) ★Grade A — EPA 2g/day attenuates muscle wastingFish oil supplement. EPA preferentially over DHA. Liquid form for poor appetite.
Oral nutritional supplementsGrade B — improves intake and QoLHigh-calorie, high-protein. Fortisip, Ensure Plus. Small volume, high density.
Resistance exerciseGrade B — anabolic stimulus, preserves muscle2–3x/week even low intensity. Supervised initially.
Megestrol acetateImproves appetite — conventional optionDVT/PE risk. Short-term. Consult oncologist.
🟡 EPA — the anti-cachectic omega-3
Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), not DHA, is the key anti-cachectic omega-3. Mechanism: ↓ PIF (proteolysis-inducing factor), ↓ NF-κB, ↓ muscle ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Dose: 2g EPA/day from fish oil. Clinical trials show stabilisation of weight and lean mass in pancreatic and lung cancer cachexia. Liquid fish oil is better tolerated when appetite is poor.

cards.title: Cancer & Sleep — Managing Treatment-Related Insomnia

rows: Take steroids before 12pm if possible. Tart cherry juice (melatonin precursor). L-theanine 200mg.
Acupuncture ★ (Grade A), black cohosh, venlafaxine (conventional). Cool bedroom.
Optimise analgesia timing. Melatonin 3–10mg for pain modulation (acts on pain pathways). Yoga nidra.
CBT-I + mindfulness. MBSR (Grade A for cancer anxiety). Ashwagandha 600mg (if not on anticoagulants).
Chemo fatigue paradox (exhausted but can't sleep)
Moderate aerobic exercise ★ (Grade A) — most evidence. Restrict caffeine to before 12pm.
Ginger tea before bed, acupressure P6 point. Small dry crackers at bedside. Ondansetron if prescribed.

pearls.label: 🟡 Melatonin in cancer — dual role
pearls.text: Melatonin serves dual roles: (1) sleep regulation — 3–10mg 30 min before bed, (2) oncostatic — radiosensitiser, anti-proliferative, antioxidant, immune-modulatory at 20–40mg doses. Grade B evidence for QoL improvement. Safe with most regimens — check with oncologist for hormone-sensitive cancers. Always start with 3mg and titrate up over 2–4 weeks.

cards._legacyHtml:
Cause of insomnia in cancerNaturopathic approach
Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) — very commonTake steroids before 12pm if possible. Tart cherry juice (melatonin precursor). L-theanine 200mg.
Hot flushes (hormone therapy)Acupuncture ★ (Grade A), black cohosh, venlafaxine (conventional). Cool bedroom.
Pain-related wakingOptimise analgesia timing. Melatonin 3–10mg for pain modulation (acts on pain pathways). Yoga nidra.
Anxiety and fearCBT-I + mindfulness. MBSR (Grade A for cancer anxiety). Ashwagandha 600mg (if not on anticoagulants).
Chemo fatigue paradox (exhausted but can't sleep)Moderate aerobic exercise ★ (Grade A) — most evidence. Restrict caffeine to before 12pm.
Nausea (overnight)Ginger tea before bed, acupressure P6 point. Small dry crackers at bedside. Ondansetron if prescribed.
🟡 Melatonin in cancer — dual role
Melatonin serves dual roles: (1) sleep regulation — 3–10mg 30 min before bed, (2) oncostatic — radiosensitiser, anti-proliferative, antioxidant, immune-modulatory at 20–40mg doses. Grade B evidence for QoL improvement. Safe with most regimens — check with oncologist for hormone-sensitive cancers. Always start with 3mg and titrate up over 2–4 weeks.

cards.title: Integrative Oncology — Cancer Type Protocols

rows: Exercise (↓ recurrence 40%), vitamin D (if deficient), MBSR, acupuncture (hot flushes, CINV), soy food (safe — not supplements)
High-dose phytoestrogens, high-dose antioxidants during chemo, red clover supplements
Exercise ★ (↓ recurrence 50%), Mediterranean diet, fibre, turmeric/curcumin (adjunct), probiotics during chemo
High-dose folic acid (may promote cell division), NSAIDs chronically without supervision
Exercise, lycopene (tomato-rich diet), pomegranate juice (PSA kinetics), soy food, selenium, vitamin D
Exercise, vitamin D (if deficient), ginger (CINV), acupuncture (dyspnoea, pain), omega-3 (cachexia prevention)
High-dose beta-carotene ★ (Caret trial — 28% ↑ lung cancer risk in smokers)
Exercise (gentle, HRV-guided), probiotics (if on antibiotics), ginger (CINV), vitamin D
High-dose vitamin C IV (without oncologist consent), any immunostimulant herbs with stem cell transplant

Cancer typeBest-evidenced integrative interventionsAvoid
Breast cancer ★Exercise (↓ recurrence 40%), vitamin D (if deficient), MBSR, acupuncture (hot flushes, CINV), soy food (safe — not supplements)High-dose phytoestrogens, high-dose antioxidants during chemo, red clover supplements
Colorectal cancerExercise ★ (↓ recurrence 50%), Mediterranean diet, fibre, turmeric/curcumin (adjunct), probiotics during chemoHigh-dose folic acid (may promote cell division), NSAIDs chronically without supervision
Prostate cancerExercise, lycopene (tomato-rich diet), pomegranate juice (PSA kinetics), soy food, selenium, vitamin DHigh-dose zinc >100mg (may promote prostate cancer)
Lung cancerExercise, vitamin D (if deficient), ginger (CINV), acupuncture (dyspnoea, pain), omega-3 (cachexia prevention)High-dose beta-carotene ★ (Caret trial — 28% ↑ lung cancer risk in smokers)
HaematologicalExercise (gentle, HRV-guided), probiotics (if on antibiotics), ginger (CINV), vitamin DHigh-dose vitamin C IV (without oncologist consent), any immunostimulant herbs with stem cell transplant

cards.title: Chemotherapy Side Effect Management

alerts.label: ⚠️ All naturopathic support during chemotherapy requires oncologist approval
alerts.text: Some supplements interact with chemotherapy — either reducing efficacy (antioxidants during certain regimens) or increasing toxicity. Always disclose to the oncology team. General principle: focus on non-supplement naturopathic interventions first (exercise, diet, mind-body), then discuss supplement support with the team.

rows: Acupuncture PC6 ★★★ (endorsed by ASCO and SIO), ginger 250mg QID ★★★ (comparable to metoclopramide for mild CINV), P6 acupressure bands (Sea-Bands)
Glutamine oral rinse 1–2g in water 4×/day ★★★, manuka honey UMF10+ coating ★★★, cryotherapy (ice chips during infusion for 5-FU) ★★★, gentle saline rinses, avoid spicy/acid foods
Exercise ★★★ (most evidence-based intervention — walking 30 min/day), yoga ★★★, acupuncture ★★, cognitive adaptation techniques, pacing strategy
Alpha-lipoic acid 600mg IV/oral ★★★, B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg ★★★, acetyl-L-carnitine 2g ★★, omega-3 3g ★★, glovestocking exercises, heat therapy

Mushroom beta-glucans (reishi, turkey tail — PSK ★★★ Japanese oncology standard), astragalus ★★ (stimulates G-CSF), Vit D ★★, probiotics for GI immunity (LGG, Saccharomyces). Avoid: Echinacea (may interfere with immunosuppressive chemo)
★★★ PSK (turkey tail) — Japanese oncology adjunct for 30+ years
Acupuncture ★★★ (Grade A — SIO endorsed), venlafaxine equivalent (conventional), black cohosh controversial in ER+ (discuss with oncologist), mindfulness ★★
★★★ Acupuncture for hot flushes in breast cancer

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ All naturopathic support during chemotherapy requires oncologist approval
Some supplements interact with chemotherapy — either reducing efficacy (antioxidants during certain regimens) or increasing toxicity. Always disclose to the oncology team. General principle: focus on non-supplement naturopathic interventions first (exercise, diet, mind-body), then discuss supplement support with the team.
Side EffectNaturopathic InterventionEvidence
Nausea/vomiting (CINV)Acupuncture PC6 ★★★ (endorsed by ASCO and SIO), ginger 250mg QID ★★★ (comparable to metoclopramide for mild CINV), P6 acupressure bands (Sea-Bands)★★★ PC6 acupuncture — Cochrane Grade A
Mucositis (oral)Glutamine oral rinse 1–2g in water 4×/day ★★★, manuka honey UMF10+ coating ★★★, cryotherapy (ice chips during infusion for 5-FU) ★★★, gentle saline rinses, avoid spicy/acid foods★★★ Glutamine RCT (grade 2 mucositis prevention)
Cancer-related fatigueExercise ★★★ (most evidence-based intervention — walking 30 min/day), yoga ★★★, acupuncture ★★, cognitive adaptation techniques, pacing strategy★★★ Exercise meta-analysis: 27% fatigue reduction
Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN)Alpha-lipoic acid 600mg IV/oral ★★★, B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg ★★★, acetyl-L-carnitine 2g ★★, omega-3 3g ★★, glovestocking exercises, heat therapy★★★ ALA for CIPN — multiple RCTs
Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia)Mushroom beta-glucans (reishi, turkey tail — PSK ★★★ Japanese oncology standard), astragalus ★★ (stimulates G-CSF), Vit D ★★, probiotics for GI immunity (LGG, Saccharomyces). Avoid: Echinacea (may interfere with immunosuppressive chemo)★★★ PSK (turkey tail) — Japanese oncology adjunct for 30+ years
Hot flushes (hormonal therapy)Acupuncture ★★★ (Grade A — SIO endorsed), venlafaxine equivalent (conventional), black cohosh controversial in ER+ (discuss with oncologist), mindfulness ★★★★★ Acupuncture for hot flushes in breast cancer

cards.title: Cancer Survivorship — Long-Term Naturopathic

alerts.label: Cancer Survivorship — A New Naturopathic Frontier
alerts.text: With cancer survival rates improving (5-year survival now 68% overall), there are more cancer survivors than ever. Survivorship care addresses: long-term treatment side effects, psychological adaptation, secondary prevention, and health promotion. BNYS practitioners are ideally positioned for the prevention-focused, holistic survivorship care model.

rows: Graded exercise ★★★ (must be gradual), Vit D correction, B12, thyroid screening (radiation-induced hypothyroidism), CoQ10, sleep optimisation, MBSR ★★★
★★★ Exercise most effective — reduces long-term cancer fatigue
Exercise ★★★ (BDNF), omega-3 DHA 2g ★★, lion's mane ★★, cognitive training, sleep priority, manage depression, estrogen normalisation (if hormone-related)
★★ Emerging evidence for omega-3 + exercise combination
Ca 1000mg + D3 4000IU + K2 MK-7 180mcg ★★★ (mandatory with AI therapy), resistance training, denosumab if significant bone loss (conventional)
★★★ Ca+D3 protocol in AI-treated breast cancer
Mediterranean diet ★★★ (reduces breast cancer recurrence in PREDIMED-Plus). Caloric deficit if overweight. Exercise 150 min/week ★★★. Avoid: obesity increases oestrogen and cancer recurrence risk.
★★★ Multiple RCTs linking exercise + Mediterranean diet to recurrence reduction
MBSR 8-week ★★★ (reduces cancer distress, fatigue, improves QoL), yoga ★★★, ACT (acceptance and commitment therapy), fear of recurrence management (specific CBT protocol), support groups
★★★ MBSR in cancer survivorship — landmark Carlson RCTs

Maintain healthy weight ★★★, exercise 150+ min/week ★★★, Mediterranean diet, adequate sleep, avoid alcohol, eliminate smoking. For hormone-sensitive cancers: phytoestrogen caution — discuss with oncologist (soy safe, high-dose isoflavone supplements — discuss)
★★★ Lifestyle factors reduce recurrence 30–50% across cancer types

Post-treatment fatigue syndromeGraded exercise ★★★ (must be gradual), Vit D correction, B12, thyroid screening (radiation-induced hypothyroidism), CoQ10, sleep optimisation, MBSR ★★★★★★ Exercise most effective — reduces long-term cancer fatigue

Avoid: obesity increases oestrogen and cancer recurrence risk.★★★ Multiple RCTs linking exercise + Mediterranean diet to recurrence reduction

cards.title: Palliative Naturopathy — Symptom Management

alerts.label: Palliative Naturopathy — Dignity and Comfort at Every Stage
alerts.text: Palliative care is not about giving up — it is about maximising quality of life, dignity, and comfort at every stage of serious illness. BNYS practitioners can contribute meaningfully to palliative care through: symptom management, family support, spiritual support, and ensuring the patient retains agency and naturopathic identity through illness.

rows: Acupuncture ★★★ (analgesic — reduce opioid requirement), massage ★★ (comfort, connective touch), TENS ★★, topical arnica/capsaicin, heat therapy. Hydrotherapy: warm baths for bone metastasis pain.
Work within palliative care team. Never withhold opioids when needed. Acupuncture reduces opioid requirements by 30% in cancer pain (meta-analysis)
Handheld fan to face ★★★ (vagal reflex reduces dyspnoea sensation even without SpO2 change), Pranayama (slow 4-count exhale), positioning (sitting forward, supported arms). Oxygen only if SpO2 <90%.
Fan therapy is remarkably effective and evidence-based for dyspnoea at end of life. Ensure family knows this technique.

Small, frequent, favourite foods. High-calorie density (avocado, nuts, healthy oils, protein powder). Ginger for nausea ★★★. Megestrol (medical). Cannabis (antiemetic, appetite stimulation — where legal). Social meals.
Do not force food near end of life — it is natural for appetite to diminish. Focus on pleasure and social connection of eating, not caloric targets.

Senna (stimulant laxative — first choice for opioid constipation ★★★). Osmotic laxatives (lactulose, PEG). Probiotics. Abdominal massage ★★. Adequate hydration (if able). Positioning (squat-like). Macrogol for prophylaxis when starting opioids.
Opioid-induced constipation does not respond to fibre alone — needs stimulant laxative. Start laxative with EVERY opioid prescription.

Yoga Nidra ★★★ (reduces existential distress in RCT at end of life — Samueli Institute). Dignity therapy (recording life narrative). Meaning-centred therapy. Chaplaincy/spiritual care. Mindfulness — focusing on present experience. Art therapy, music therapy.
Spiritual care is legitimate medicine — ask about meaning, fear, unfinished business, legacy. "What matters most to you now?" is the most important end-of-life question.

Plant-rich diet↓ Colorectal cancer (fibre), ↓ processed meat associationButyrate, phytochemicals, transit time ↓
Cruciferous vegetables↓ Lung, colon, breast cancer; indole-3-carbinol modulates oestrogenSulforaphane (Nrf2), I3C → DIM (oestrogen metabolism)
Soy (isoflavones)Protective in breast Ca (population data) — controversial in BRCA+SERM-like activity — context dependent
Turmeric/curcuminPre-clinical strong, clinical trials ongoing; safe adjunctNF-κB, apoptosis, anti-angiogenic
Caloric restriction / fastingFasting 24–72h before chemo ↓ side effects, ↑ efficacy (pilot data)IGF-1 ↓, differential stress response (normal vs cancer cells)
🟡 What to avoid in oncology
High-dose antioxidants (Vit C, Vit E, beta-carotene) during chemotherapy — may protect cancer cells from oxidative damage of chemo/RT. St John's Wort with any chemotherapy — CYP3A4 induction reduces drug levels. High-dose green tea extract with bortezomib (direct antagonism — clinical trial stopped). Always check herb-chemo interactions at oncologydatabaseinteraction.com or Memorial Sloan Kettering's About Herbs database.

cards.title: Herb & Supplement Safety in Cancer Treatment

alerts.label: Critical rule
alerts.text: ALL high-dose antioxidant supplements should be paused 48 hours before and during chemotherapy/radiotherapy unless specifically approved by the oncologist. This is the single most important safety rule in integrative oncology.

rows: May antagonise oxidative mechanism of some chemo drugs. Also possibly synergistic — highly context-dependent. Do NOT recommend without oncologist consent.
CYP3A4 induction → ↓ irinotecan, imatinib, tamoxifen, taxol levels by 30–50%
Antagonises bortezomib (boronic acid-EGCG binding). Moderate doses of green tea as beverage: safe.
Theoretical oestrogenic effect in hormone-sensitive breast/endo cancer — avoid supplements; dietary soy appears safe
Generally safe, potentially synergistic (radiosensitiser) — check with oncologist

cards._legacyHtml:
Critical rule
ALL high-dose antioxidant supplements should be paused 48 hours before and during chemotherapy/radiotherapy unless specifically approved by the oncologist. This is the single most important safety rule in integrative oncology.
SupplementConcern with chemotherapyEvidence level
Vitamin C (high-dose IV/oral)May antagonise oxidative mechanism of some chemo drugs. Also possibly synergistic — highly context-dependent. Do NOT recommend without oncologist consent.Conflicting
St John's WortCYP3A4 induction → ↓ irinotecan, imatinib, tamoxifen, taxol levels by 30–50%Contraindicated ★
Green tea extract (EGCG)Antagonises bortezomib (boronic acid-EGCG binding). Moderate doses of green tea as beverage: safe.Avoid with bortezomib
Soy isoflavonesTheoretical oestrogenic effect in hormone-sensitive breast/endo cancer — avoid supplements; dietary soy appears safeDietary safe, supplements avoid
MelatoninGenerally safe, potentially synergistic (radiosensitiser) — check with oncologistLikely safe

cards.title: Integrative Oncology — Cancer Type Protocols

rows: Exercise (↓ recurrence 40%), vitamin D (if deficient), MBSR, acupuncture (hot flushes, CINV), soy food (safe — not supplements)
High-dose phytoestrogens, high-dose antioxidants during chemo, red clover supplements
Exercise ★ (↓ recurrence 50%), Mediterranean diet, fibre, turmeric/curcumin (adjunct), probiotics during chemo
High-dose folic acid (may promote cell division), NSAIDs chronically without supervision
Exercise, lycopene (tomato-rich diet), pomegranate juice (PSA kinetics), soy food, selenium, vitamin D
Exercise, vitamin D (if deficient), ginger (CINV), acupuncture (dyspnoea, pain), omega-3 (cachexia prevention)
High-dose beta-carotene ★ (Caret trial — 28% ↑ lung cancer risk in smokers)
Exercise (gentle, HRV-guided), probiotics (if on antibiotics), ginger (CINV), vitamin D
High-dose vitamin C IV (without oncologist consent), any immunostimulant herbs with stem cell transplant

cards._legacyHtml:
Cancer typeBest-evidenced integrative interventionsAvoid
Breast cancer ★Exercise (↓ recurrence 40%), vitamin D (if deficient), MBSR, acupuncture (hot flushes, CINV), soy food (safe — not supplements)High-dose phytoestrogens, high-dose antioxidants during chemo, red clover supplements
Colorectal cancerExercise ★ (↓ recurrence 50%), Mediterranean diet, fibre, turmeric/curcumin (adjunct), probiotics during chemoHigh-dose folic acid (may promote cell division), NSAIDs chronically without supervision
Prostate cancerExercise, lycopene (tomato-rich diet), pomegranate juice (PSA kinetics), soy food, selenium, vitamin DHigh-dose zinc >100mg (may promote prostate cancer)
Lung cancerExercise, vitamin D (if deficient), ginger (CINV), acupuncture (dyspnoea, pain), omega-3 (cachexia prevention)High-dose beta-carotene ★ (Caret trial — 28% ↑ lung cancer risk in smokers)
HaematologicalExercise (gentle, HRV-guided), probiotics (if on antibiotics), ginger (CINV), vitamin DHigh-dose vitamin C IV (without oncologist consent), any immunostimulant herbs with stem cell transplant

cards.title: Cancer Cachexia — Naturopathic Nutritional Support

alerts.label: Definition
alerts.text: Cancer cachexia = metabolic syndrome of muscle wasting (sarcopenia), anorexia, inflammation and fat loss driven by tumour-host interactions. Present in 80% of advanced cancer. Accounts for 20–30% of cancer deaths directly.

rows: Weight loss >5% (or BMI <20 + >2% loss, or sarcopenia + >2% loss)
Performance status 3–4, progressive disease, life expectancy <3 months
Grade A — 1.5–2.0g/kg/day to preserve lean mass
Leucine-rich sources (whey protein, legumes, fish). Small frequent meals.
Grade A — EPA 2g/day attenuates muscle wasting
Fish oil supplement. EPA preferentially over DHA. Liquid form for poor appetite.
Grade B — improves intake and QoL
High-calorie, high-protein. Fortisip, Ensure Plus. Small volume, high density.
Grade B — anabolic stimulus, preserves muscle

pearls.label: 🟡 EPA — the anti-cachectic omega-3
pearls.text: Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), not DHA, is the key anti-cachectic omega-3. Mechanism: ↓ PIF (proteolysis-inducing factor), ↓ NF-κB, ↓ muscle ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Dose: 2g EPA/day from fish oil. Clinical trials show stabilisation of weight and lean mass in pancreatic and lung cancer cachexia. Liquid fish oil is better tolerated when appetite is poor.

cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Cancer cachexia = metabolic syndrome of muscle wasting (sarcopenia), anorexia, inflammation and fat loss driven by tumour-host interactions. Present in 80% of advanced cancer. Accounts for 20–30% of cancer deaths directly.
StageFeaturesIntervention window
Pre-cachexiaWeight loss <5%, anorexia, metabolic changesBest intervention window — preventive
CachexiaWeight loss >5% (or BMI <20 + >2% loss, or sarcopenia + >2% loss)Multimodal — nutrition + exercise + pharmacology
Refractory cachexiaPerformance status 3–4, progressive disease, life expectancy <3 monthsComfort and dignity focus
Nutritional interventionEvidenceProtocol
Protein ★Grade A — 1.5–2.0g/kg/day to preserve lean massLeucine-rich sources (whey protein, legumes, fish). Small frequent meals.
Omega-3 (EPA) ★Grade A — EPA 2g/day attenuates muscle wastingFish oil supplement. EPA preferentially over DHA. Liquid form for poor appetite.
Oral nutritional supplementsGrade B — improves intake and QoLHigh-calorie, high-protein. Fortisip, Ensure Plus. Small volume, high density.
Resistance exerciseGrade B — anabolic stimulus, preserves muscle2–3x/week even low intensity. Supervised initially.
Megestrol acetateImproves appetite — conventional optionDVT/PE risk. Short-term. Consult oncologist.
🟡 EPA — the anti-cachectic omega-3
Eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), not DHA, is the key anti-cachectic omega-3. Mechanism: ↓ PIF (proteolysis-inducing factor), ↓ NF-κB, ↓ muscle ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Dose: 2g EPA/day from fish oil. Clinical trials show stabilisation of weight and lean mass in pancreatic and lung cancer cachexia. Liquid fish oil is better tolerated when appetite is poor.

cards.title: Cancer & Sleep — Managing Treatment-Related Insomnia

rows: Take steroids before 12pm if possible. Tart cherry juice (melatonin precursor). L-theanine 200mg.
Acupuncture ★ (Grade A), black cohosh, venlafaxine (conventional). Cool bedroom.
Optimise analgesia timing. Melatonin 3–10mg for pain modulation (acts on pain pathways). Yoga nidra.
CBT-I + mindfulness. MBSR (Grade A for cancer anxiety). Ashwagandha 600mg (if not on anticoagulants).
Chemo fatigue paradox (exhausted but can't sleep)
Moderate aerobic exercise ★ (Grade A) — most evidence. Restrict caffeine to before 12pm.
Ginger tea before bed, acupressure P6 point. Small dry crackers at bedside. Ondansetron if prescribed.

pearls.label: 🟡 Melatonin in cancer — dual role
pearls.text: Melatonin serves dual roles: (1) sleep regulation — 3–10mg 30 min before bed, (2) oncostatic — radiosensitiser, anti-proliferative, antioxidant, immune-modulatory at 20–40mg doses. Grade B evidence for QoL improvement. Safe with most regimens — check with oncologist for hormone-sensitive cancers. Always start with 3mg and titrate up over 2–4 weeks.

Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) — very commonTake steroids before 12pm if possible. Tart cherry juice (melatonin precursor). L-theanine 200mg.

🟡 Melatonin in cancer — dual role
Melatonin serves dual roles: (1) sleep regulation — 3–10mg 30 min before bed, (2) oncostatic — radiosensitiser, anti-proliferative, antioxidant, immune-modulatory at 20–40mg doses. Grade B evidence for QoL improvement. Safe with most regimens — check with oncologist for hormone-sensitive cancers. Always start with 3mg and titrate up over 2–4 weeks.

cards.title: Chemotherapy Side Effect Management

alerts.label: ⚠️ All naturopathic support during chemotherapy requires oncologist approval
alerts.text: Some supplements interact with chemotherapy — either reducing efficacy (antioxidants during certain regimens) or increasing toxicity. Always disclose to the oncology team. General principle: focus on non-supplement naturopathic interventions first (exercise, diet, mind-body), then discuss supplement support with the team.

rows: Acupuncture PC6 ★★★ (endorsed by ASCO and SIO), ginger 250mg QID ★★★ (comparable to metoclopramide for mild CINV), P6 acupressure bands (Sea-Bands)
Glutamine oral rinse 1–2g in water 4×/day ★★★, manuka honey UMF10+ coating ★★★, cryotherapy (ice chips during infusion for 5-FU) ★★★, gentle saline rinses, avoid spicy/acid foods
Exercise ★★★ (most evidence-based intervention — walking 30 min/day), yoga ★★★, acupuncture ★★, cognitive adaptation techniques, pacing strategy
Alpha-lipoic acid 600mg IV/oral ★★★, B12 methylcobalamin 1000mcg ★★★, acetyl-L-carnitine 2g ★★, omega-3 3g ★★, glovestocking exercises, heat therapy

Mushroom beta-glucans (reishi, turkey tail — PSK ★★★ Japanese oncology standard), astragalus ★★ (stimulates G-CSF), Vit D ★★, probiotics for GI immunity (LGG, Saccharomyces). Avoid: Echinacea (may interfere with immunosuppressive chemo)
★★★ PSK (turkey tail) — Japanese oncology adjunct for 30+ years
Acupuncture ★★★ (Grade A — SIO endorsed), venlafaxine equivalent (conventional), black cohosh controversial in ER+ (discuss with oncologist), mindfulness ★★
★★★ Acupuncture for hot flushes in breast cancer

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ All naturopathic support during chemotherapy requires oncologist approval
Some supplements interact with chemotherapy — either reducing efficacy (antioxidants during certain regimens) or increasing toxicity. Always disclose to the oncology team. General principle: focus on non-supplement naturopathic interventions first (exercise, diet, mind-body), then discuss supplement support with the team.

Mucositis (oral)Glutamine oral rinse 1–2g in water 4×/day ★★★, manuka honey UMF10+ coating ★★★, cryotherapy (ice chips during infusion for 5-FU) ★★★, gentle saline rinses, avoid spicy/acid foods★★★ Glutamine RCT (grade 2 mucositis prevention)
Cancer-related fatigueExercise ★★★ (most evidence-based intervention — walking 30 min/day), yoga ★★★, acupuncture ★★, cognitive adaptation techniques, pacing strategy★★★ Exercise meta-analysis: 27% fatigue reduction

Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia)Mushroom beta-glucans (reishi, turkey tail — PSK ★★★ Japanese oncology standard), astragalus ★★ (stimulates G-CSF), Vit D ★★, probiotics for GI immunity (LGG, Saccharomyces). Avoid: Echinacea (may interfere with immunosuppressive chemo)★★★ PSK (turkey tail) — Japanese oncology adjunct for 30+ years

cards.title: Cancer Survivorship — Long-Term Naturopathic

alerts.label: Cancer Survivorship — A New Naturopathic Frontier
alerts.text: With cancer survival rates improving (5-year survival now 68% overall), there are more cancer survivors than ever. Survivorship care addresses: long-term treatment side effects, psychological adaptation, secondary prevention, and health promotion. BNYS practitioners are ideally positioned for the prevention-focused, holistic survivorship care model.

rows: Graded exercise ★★★ (must be gradual), Vit D correction, B12, thyroid screening (radiation-induced hypothyroidism), CoQ10, sleep optimisation, MBSR ★★★
★★★ Exercise most effective — reduces long-term cancer fatigue
Exercise ★★★ (BDNF), omega-3 DHA 2g ★★, lion's mane ★★, cognitive training, sleep priority, manage depression, estrogen normalisation (if hormone-related)
★★ Emerging evidence for omega-3 + exercise combination
Ca 1000mg + D3 4000IU + K2 MK-7 180mcg ★★★ (mandatory with AI therapy), resistance training, denosumab if significant bone loss (conventional)
★★★ Ca+D3 protocol in AI-treated breast cancer
Mediterranean diet ★★★ (reduces breast cancer recurrence in PREDIMED-Plus). Caloric deficit if overweight. Exercise 150 min/week ★★★. Avoid: obesity increases oestrogen and cancer recurrence risk.
★★★ Multiple RCTs linking exercise + Mediterranean diet to recurrence reduction
MBSR 8-week ★★★ (reduces cancer distress, fatigue, improves QoL), yoga ★★★, ACT (acceptance and commitment therapy), fear of recurrence management (specific CBT protocol), support groups
★★★ MBSR in cancer survivorship — landmark Carlson RCTs

Maintain healthy weight ★★★, exercise 150+ min/week ★★★, Mediterranean diet, adequate sleep, avoid alcohol, eliminate smoking. For hormone-sensitive cancers: phytoestrogen caution — discuss with oncologist (soy safe, high-dose isoflavone supplements — discuss)
★★★ Lifestyle factors reduce recurrence 30–50% across cancer types

cards.title: Palliative Naturopathy — Symptom Management

alerts.label: Palliative Naturopathy — Dignity and Comfort at Every Stage
alerts.text: Palliative care is not about giving up — it is about maximising quality of life, dignity, and comfort at every stage of serious illness. BNYS practitioners can contribute meaningfully to palliative care through: symptom management, family support, spiritual support, and ensuring the patient retains agency and naturopathic identity through illness.

rows: Acupuncture ★★★ (analgesic — reduce opioid requirement), massage ★★ (comfort, connective touch), TENS ★★, topical arnica/capsaicin, heat therapy. Hydrotherapy: warm baths for bone metastasis pain.
Work within palliative care team. Never withhold opioids when needed. Acupuncture reduces opioid requirements by 30% in cancer pain (meta-analysis)
Handheld fan to face ★★★ (vagal reflex reduces dyspnoea sensation even without SpO2 change), Pranayama (slow 4-count exhale), positioning (sitting forward, supported arms). Oxygen only if SpO2 <90%.
Fan therapy is remarkably effective and evidence-based for dyspnoea at end of life. Ensure family knows this technique.

Small, frequent, favourite foods. High-calorie density (avocado, nuts, healthy oils, protein powder). Ginger for nausea ★★★. Megestrol (medical). Cannabis (antiemetic, appetite stimulation — where legal). Social meals.
Do not force food near end of life — it is natural for appetite to diminish. Focus on pleasure and social connection of eating, not caloric targets.

Senna (stimulant laxative — first choice for opioid constipation ★★★). Osmotic laxatives (lactulose, PEG). Probiotics. Abdominal massage ★★. Adequate hydration (if able). Positioning (squat-like). Macrogol for prophylaxis when starting opioids.
Opioid-induced constipation does not respond to fibre alone — needs stimulant laxative. Start laxative with EVERY opioid prescription.

Yoga Nidra ★★★ (reduces existential distress in RCT at end of life — Samueli Institute). Dignity therapy (recording life narrative). Meaning-centred therapy. Chaplaincy/spiritual care. Mindfulness — focusing on present experience. Art therapy, music therapy.
Spiritual care is legitimate medicine — ask about meaning, fear, unfinished business, legacy. "What matters most to you now?" is the most important end-of-life question.

Palliative care is not about giving up — it is about maximising quality of life, dignity, and comfort at every stage of serious illness.
